draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from a high-contrast, vibrant color palette and dynamic typography that mirrors the physical energy of running and keeps the runner motivated and alert.
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool, energetic vs calm
facade.label: Bright and energetic
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use saturated colors, bold fonts, and animated transitions. Visual language should feel alive and propulsive, matching runner psychology.
facade.rejectImplies: The app should favor muted tones, gentle transitions, and a quieter visual presence. The mood should be contemplative and grounding rather than stimulating.

evidence.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from a high-contrast, vibrant color palette and dynamic typography that mirrors the physical energy of running and keeps the runner motivated and alert.
evidence.implication: The app should use saturated colors, bold fonts, and animated transitions. Visual language should feel alive and propulsive, matching runner psychology.

facade.hypothesis: A friendly, conversational voice (like texting a running buddy) will feel more approachable than formal, technical language for a running app.
facade.label: Casual tone
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into conversational language, humor, and a peer-to-peer tone. Avoid corporate or instructional copy.
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward polished, authoritative, or minimal language. Prioritize clarity and expert credibility over warmth.

facade.hypothesis: Users building habit (running) prefer clean, distraction-free design that feels energetic rather than dark or complex
facade.axisTargeted: Light vs dark; Simple vs detailed visual density
facade.label: Bright, minimal interface
facade.acceptImplies: Push toward high contrast, lots of white space, bold accent colors for data (pace, distance, time)
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer moodier tones, more layered information density, or softer visual hierarchy

facade.hypothesis: Runners want real-time metrics (pace, HR, distance) visible simultaneously with map/route, rather than switching between tabs or layers
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data vs key metrics — whether to show full telemetry at once or surface only critical stats
facade.label: Split screen live data
facade.acceptImplies: User values glanceable multi-metric feedback during active run; design should prioritize parallel info streams and dashboard density
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers focused, minimal display with one primary metric; design should hide secondary data behind taps or favor narrative (story of run) over live numbers

facade.hypothesis: Runners expect metrics to appear gradually during their run rather than pop instantly, creating a breathing, human-paced reveal of information.
facade.axisTargeted: Animation timing: gradual emergence vs instant appearance
facade.label: Run data fades in
facade.acceptImplies: Other interface elements during active runs should use fade-in or slide animations; data feels less jarring when discovered gradually.
facade.rejectImplies: Instant, snappy toggles and stat displays are preferred; runners want immediate feedback and don't want to wait for information to materialize.

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left sidebar with quick stats (temp, pace, distance) stays on-screen during runs, with main content (map/route) taking the right space
facade.axisTargeted: Layout structure: sidebar persistence vs collapsible/hidden nav
facade.label: Sidebar always visible
facade.acceptImplies: User values constant access to key metrics without tapping; favors split-screen layouts; prioritizes information density and quick glances
facade.rejectImplies: User wants full-screen immersion for map/route; prefers content-first design; sidebar feels cramped or distracting during active running

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive menu navigation for weather info during runs
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants energizing, action-oriented feel vs. calm, meditative atmosphere
Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV)
axes.poleA: Just the basics or all the data

axes.label: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback in app interactions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive menu navigation for weather info during runs
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants energizing, action-oriented feel vs. calm, meditative atmosphere
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback in app interactions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from a restful, uncluttered interface with soft colors, gentle typography, and ample whitespace that reduces cognitive load before and after runs, allowing users to focus on their performance data without visual noise.
facade.label: Calm and minimal
facade.acceptImplies: The app should embrace a serene aesthetic with muted earth tones, generous spacing, and subtle animations. Visual language prioritizes clarity and tranquility over excitement.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want visual stimulation and energy in their running app; tranquility may feel boring or unmotivating for an activity-focused tool.

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
